era_context: Late 14th century: The Western Schism begins with competing papal elections. The Ming Dynasty consolidates power in China. The Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans. The Hundred Years' War continues in Europe. African, Asian, and American civilizations develop independently of European awareness.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented)
Southeast Asia (sparse European sources; local chronicles underrepresented)
Americas (no European contact; indigenous records limited)

events.title: Western Schism begins: Urban VI elected Pope; cardinals dispute legitimacy
events.description: On 8 April 1378, Bartolomeo Prignano was elected Pope Urban VI in Rome. The election was contested by French cardinals who claimed coercion by the Roman mob and the Roman populace's demand for an Italian pope. Urban VI's abrasive personality and attempts to reform the papal court alienated many cardinals. In September 1378 (or early 1379 by some accounts), a group of cardinals elected Clement VII (Robert of Geneva) as antipope, claiming Urban's election was invalid. This initiated the Western Schism, which would divide Catholic Christendom for nearly 40 years, with different regions recognizing different popes based on political allegiance. The schism weakened papal authority and contributed to the later conciliar movement.
key_figures: Catherine of Siena (mystic and papal advisor)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Western Schism origins

events.certainty_note: The papal election of Urban VI on 8 April 1378 is documented in contemporary papal records. The cardinals' subsequent dispute and Clement VII's election are attested in multiple near-contemporary sources. The exact date of Clement's election (September 1378 or early 1379) varies by source, but the schism's initiation in 1378 is secure.
cross_references: 1377-001 (Gregory XI's death and return of papacy to Rome)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor purges Hu Weiyong and associates; centralizes power
events.description: In 1378, the Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) executed the powerful chancellor Hu Weiyong and his associates on charges of treason and corruption. This purge was part of the emperor's broader campaign to eliminate potential rivals and centralize imperial authority. Hu Weiyong had been one of the most influential officials in the early Ming court, and his removal marked a turning point in Ming governance: the emperor abolished the position of chancellor and consolidated power directly under the throne. This set a precedent for Ming authoritarianism and reduced the influence of the bureaucratic elite. Thousands of officials and their families were executed in the purges that followed.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Ming court records and the Ming Shilu document Hu Weiyong's execution and the subsequent purges. The date and basic facts are well-established, though casualty figures vary by source.

events.title: Tokhtamysh defeats Timur at the Battle of the Syr Darya
events.description: In 1378, Tokhtamysh, Khan of the Golden Horde, defeated Timur (Tamerlane) in a battle near the Syr Darya River in Central Asia. This was one of the few significant military setbacks Timur experienced during his rise to power. However, Tokhtamysh's victory was temporary; Timur would recover and eventually dominate Central Asia, while Tokhtamysh's power would decline. The battle illustrates the complex and shifting power dynamics among the Turco-Mongol khans and confederations in the late 14th century. The conflict between Timur and Tokhtamysh would continue for years, with Timur ultimately prevailing.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Timurid-Golden Horde conflicts

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in later Persian and Islamic chronicles, but contemporary documentation is sparse. The date and details vary by source. Modern scholarship generally accepts that Tokhtamysh defeated Timur in this period, but the exact year and location are debated.

events.title: Richard II becomes King of England at age 10
events.description: On 21 June 1377, Edward III's grandson Richard II became King of England following the death of his father Edward, the Black Prince (who had predeceased Edward III). Richard was only 10 years old at his accession, necessitating a regency. By 1378, the regency was firmly established, with various nobles competing for influence. Richard's minority was marked by political instability, the Peasants' Revolt (1381), and factional conflicts among the nobility. His later reign (after he reached majority) would be characterized by conflict with Parliament and ultimately his deposition in 1399.
key_figures: John of Gaunt, Duke of Lancaster (regent and uncle)

sources.name: English royal records and parliamentary rolls

events.certainty_note: Richard II's accession date (21 June 1377) is well-documented in English royal records. His age and the regency arrangements are clearly attested.

events.title: Zheng He born (future Ming admiral and explorer)
events.description: Zheng He, the famous Ming admiral and explorer, was born in 1378 (some sources give 1371). He was the son of a Muslim Hui family in Yunnan. Zheng He would later become one of the most celebrated figures in Chinese history, commanding the massive treasure voyages (1405–1433) that explored the Indian Ocean and established Ming naval supremacy. In 1378, he was an infant; his rise to prominence would come decades later under the Yongle Emperor. His birth represents the multicultural character of early Ming China and the integration of Muslim communities into the imperial system.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Historiographic consensus on Zheng He's life

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's birth year is given as 1378 in some sources and 1371 in others. The exact date is uncertain, as is common for figures born before they achieved prominence. Modern scholarship generally accepts 1371 as more likely, but 1378 is attested in some Ming records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Western Schism is sometimes portrayed as a sudden rupture caused by Urban VI's personality, but recent scholarship emphasizes the structural weaknesses in papal authority and the complex political maneuvering that preceded the formal schism. The schism's origins are more complex than a simple dispute over election validity; it reflects deeper conflicts between French and Italian cardinals and between papal and secular power. Zheng He's birth year is disputed; some sources place it in 1371 rather than 1378, and his early life is poorly documented. The Battle of the Syr Darya's date and significance are debated; some sources place it in different years or question whether it was as significant as later chronicles suggest.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1378 is uneven. European events (papal schism, English succession, Iberian politics) are well-recorded in contemporary chronicles and official records. Chinese sources are detailed but compiled later and reflect imperial ideology. Central Asian events rely on later Persian and Islamic chronicles with significant gaps in contemporary sources. Sub-Saharan African, Southeast Asian, and Pacific events are virtually absent from surviving written records. The surviving sources are heavily biased toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Court-sponsored chronicles (especially Chinese and Ottoman) may exaggerate the power and achievements of their patrons. The Western Schism's origins are interpreted differently by Catholic, Orthodox, and secular historians, reflecting theological and political biases.

graph_edges.note: Gregory XI's death and return of papacy to Rome precipitated the disputed election of Urban VI
Urban VI's election led to Clement VII's counter-election and formalization of the schism
Hu Weiyong's purge continues the Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of Ming power
Tokhtamysh's victory was temporary; Timur recovered and dominated Central Asia
Richard II's regency began with his accession in 1377 and continued through 1378
Zheng He's birth in 1378 preceded his rise to prominence and his treasure voyages
